steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The students want to determine whether Montana's RSRs caused the changes they observed. Collecting data inside Montana alone can show that changes happened, but it can't prove RSRs caused them — something else may have driven the same trends everywhere. They need a control: data from a comparable state without RSRs over the same period.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — compare new-policy premiums (Montana) vs. existing-policy premiums (non-RSR state): RSRs directly restrict existing-policy premiums, not new-policy premiums, so comparing mismatched policy types muddies the comparison. ✗
  • Choice B — compare both data types (Montana) vs. same data types from a non-RSR state, same period: this uses both pieces of data the students collected and pairs each against the analogous measure from a control state in the same window — the cleanest way to isolate the RSR effect. ✓
  • Choice C — compare Montana vs. Montana, later period: both periods are under RSRs, so there is no control and no way to attribute any difference to the regulations. ✗
  • Choice D — compare Montana insurer numbers vs. another RSR state: uses only one of the two data types and compares two RSR states, so RSRs can't be isolated as the cause. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
